required.title: [For Mentor]
required.author: Aristotle (translated by W. Rhys Roberts or George Kennedy)

suggested.title: [Watch and Analyze] Presidential debates, legal oral arguments, TED talks, commencement addresses
[Join] A competitive debate league or Toastmasters club

supplementary.title: [Read] Selected closing arguments from landmark trials

competence_markers.description: The student can write and deliver advanced speech forms — eulogy, toast, testimony, pitch — with appropriate structure and emotional calibration
The student can speak extemporaneously for 10-15 minutes on a known topic with coherent structure and effective delivery
The student can deliver a speech to a skeptical or hostile audience, handling challenges with composure and strategic response
The student can analyze a published speech with precision, identifying structure, rhetorical techniques, and storytelling strategy
The student uses narrative strategically in their own speeches — personal anecdote, case study, parable, the return
The student regularly reviews recordings of their own speaking and can identify specific areas for improvement
The student has coached at least one other person through speech preparation and delivery with specific, actionable feedback
The student has delivered at least one speech in a real-stakes context — competition, public hearing, professional presentation, or equivalent
The student can design and facilitate a speaking exercise for a small group

narration_prompts.detail: What would you want someone to say about you? Why is specificity more powerful than generality in a eulogy?

narration_prompts.prompt: Give me an extemporaneous speech on [topic you know well].
Analyze a speech you studied recently.
narration_prompts.detail: What was its structure? Where was it most effective? Where was it weakest? What rhetorical technique was most prominent?

narration_prompts.prompt: How do you handle a hostile audience?
narration_prompts.detail: What is the difference between acknowledging disagreement and conceding the point? Give me an example of each.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about coaching someone through a speech.
narration_prompts.detail: What was their main struggle? What specific feedback did you give? What changed?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the relationship between storytelling and argumentation?
narration_prompts.detail: How does narrative make an abstract argument concrete? Give an example from a speech you have studied.

cross_connections.description: Advanced rhetorical analysis, dialectical mastery, principled negotiation, and ethical influence are the intellectual engine behind advanced speaking. 3.3.6-C delivers the argument; 3.3.9-C constructs it.
Command presence — the specific combination of posture, movement, eye contact, and stillness that causes a room to orient toward you — is the physical expression of speaking mastery.
Shaping the room — changing its energy, redirecting attention, managing transitions — is what separates a speaker who delivers to a room from a speaker who transforms it.

The best speakers are performers. Dramatic training, musical performance, and artistic expression develop the vocal range, physical expressiveness, and audience engagement that elevate speaking from competent to compelling.
Leadership is exercised through speech more than through any other medium. The leader who speaks compellingly mobilizes action. The leader who speaks poorly is ignored regardless of their authority.

The ethical dimension of rhetoric — when persuasion is legitimate, when it becomes manipulation, what obligations a speaker has to truth — is a philosophical question. The advanced speaker must wrestle with it.

Rhetoric without character is manipulation. The speaker who has internalized IC1's formation uses their skill in service of truth and honest conviction. The speaker who has not is a danger to everyone who listens.

content.why_this_matters:
You can already stand up and deliver a structured speech. You can think on your feet. You can debate. Now you are going to become the kind of speaker who can walk into any room — a boardroom, a courtroom, a church, a rally, a hostile town hall meeting — and command attention through the sheer quality of what you say and how you say it.

The skills in this packet are the skills of leaders, advocates, and people who shape what happens in the world. A eulogy that honors a life. A toast that moves a room to laughter and tears. A pitch that wins funding. A testimony that changes policy. An extemporaneous address that inspires action. These are not school exercises. They are the speaking situations that define careers, communities, and movements.

You will also cross a critical threshold: from being a speaker to being someone who can develop speakers. The ability to coach others — to watch someone struggle with a speech and give them the specific feedback that unlocks their improvement — is the highest demonstration that you have internalized the skill deeply enough to transmit it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Advanced speech forms** — eulogies, toasts, testimony, commencement addresses, pitch presentations
2. **Extemporaneous mastery** — speaking for 10-20 minutes from a brief outline or no notes at all
3. **Hostile audience management** — maintaining composure and effectiveness when the audience is skeptical, disengaged, or opposed
4. **Storytelling as rhetoric** — strategic use of narrative to make abstract arguments concrete and emotionally resonant
5. **Video self-analysis** — the habit of reviewing and improving your own speaking with analytical rigor
6. **Teaching public speaking** — coaching peers through preparation and delivery, designing speaking exercises, facilitating speech practice
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Advanced Speech Forms

**The Eulogy:** Speaking about someone's life and character at their funeral or memorial. This is one of the most difficult and important speeches a person will ever give. A good eulogy is specific — not "She was a wonderful person" but "She kept a garden that fed half the neighborhood, and if you tried to refuse a bag of tomatoes, she would leave them on your porch at 6 AM." The structure: who they were (not just what they did), what they meant to the people in the room, and what endures now that they are gone. A eulogy is an act of service to the grieving. It must be honest — not a sanitized portrait, but a real one.

**The Toast:** The hardest short-form speech. A toast is brief (60-90 seconds), warm, often humorous, and ends with everyone raising a glass. The structure: a specific story or observation about the person being toasted, a genuine expression of what they mean to you, and a forward-looking wish. What makes toasts difficult: brevity demands precision. You cannot ramble for 60 seconds. Every word counts.

**Testimony:** A clear, factual, persuasive account of an experience. Used in legal, legislative, and religious contexts. The structure is radically different from a persuasive speech — testimony must be credible above all else. That means: specific details over generalizations, chronological clarity, emotional restraint (feeling is communicated through facts, not adjectives), and the ability to withstand questioning. Practice testimony by giving a factual account of an experience and having someone cross-examine you on it.

**The Pitch Presentation:** Selling an idea, a project, or a venture in a constrained time format. Structure: problem (what is broken or missing), solution (your idea), evidence (why it works), ask (what you need from the audience), vision (what success looks like). A pitch is persuasion with a deadline — you have three to ten minutes, and the audience decides at the end whether to invest, approve, or move on. Economy of language is everything.

**The Commencement Address:** Inspiring an audience around a theme. The challenge: the audience is diverse in experience and expectation, the occasion is celebratory, and the speaker must say something genuinely meaningful without being trite. The best commencement addresses are built around a single, powerful idea developed through story and example. Study David Foster Wallace's "This Is Water" and Ursula K. Le Guin's addresses as models.

### Part 2: Extemporaneous Mastery

Extemporaneous speaking — speaking at length from a brief outline or no notes at all — is the highest form of the skill. It is also the most authentic. The audience knows you are not reading. The connection is immediate and personal.

Extemporaneous mastery requires:
1. **Deep knowledge of the subject.** You cannot speak without notes on a topic you do not understand thoroughly. The notes are a crutch for incomplete understanding. When you truly know your material, the structure lives in your head.
2. **Internalized speech architecture.** The opening-body-conclusion structure must be automatic — you apply it without thinking, the way a trained musician plays chords without looking at their fingers.
3. **Trust in your own mind.** The fear of extemporaneous speaking is the fear of losing your place. You manage this by knowing that if you lose a point, you can find it again — or skip it and return. A speech is not a tightrope. It is a conversation with structure.

Practice method: Take a topic you know well. Write a three-word outline — just three words, one for each main point. Speak for ten minutes using only those three words as your guide. Record yourself. Review. Repeat with different topics until the process is natural.

### Part 3: Hostile Audiences

Not every audience wants to hear what you have to say. Some audiences are skeptical. Some are bored. Some are actively hostile — they disagree with your position and they want you to fail.

**Strategies for hostile environments:**

- **Acknowledge the disagreement.** "I know many of you disagree with what I am about to say. I respect that, and I am going to make my case. I ask only that you hear me out before you decide."
- **Find common ground first.** Before arguing the contested point, establish something you and the audience agree on. This creates a bridge: "We all want safe neighborhoods. Where we disagree is how to achieve that."
- **Stay calm.** A hostile audience wants you to become emotional, defensive, or flustered. Your composure is your authority. When you are attacked, respond with calm precision. Never match hostility with hostility — you lose the audience the moment you do.
- **Handle interruptions with grace.** "I appreciate the energy — let me finish this point, and then I want to hear from you." Do not ignore interruptions (this seems weak) or fight them (this escalates). Acknowledge, redirect, continue.
- **Do not straw-man the opposition.** If you misrepresent the opposing view in front of people who hold that view, you lose all credibility instantly. Steelman it instead: state their position more clearly than they would state it themselves, then explain why you still disagree. This earns respect even from opponents.

### Part 4: Storytelling as Rhetoric

Every great speech in history uses narrative. Lincoln at Gettysburg framed a battle as a chapter in the story of a nation dedicated to a proposition. King at the Lincoln Memorial framed civil rights as the next chapter of a promise made but not yet kept. Churchill framed a war as a story of defiance against tyranny.

**How to use story rhetorically:**

1. **The personal anecdote.** A story from your own experience that illustrates your point. Effective because it establishes ethos (you were there) and creates pathos (the audience experiences it with you). Danger: overuse makes you seem self-centered. Use sparingly.
2. **The case study.** A specific example that proves your general argument. "Food waste is a solvable problem" is an assertion. "In 2018, the city of Milan reduced food waste by 25% using a single program that cost less than the food it saved" is a story that makes the assertion credible.
3. **The parable.** A short narrative that embodies a principle. Jesus, Aesop, and every effective teacher in history used parables because stories are remembered when arguments are forgotten.
4. **The return.** Open your speech with a story. Develop your argument. Then return to the story at the end — with a new layer of meaning that the argument has created. This is the most powerful closing technique in rhetoric.

### Part 5: Teaching Speaking

The highest-order demonstration of any skill is the ability to develop it in others. When you can watch someone struggle with a speech and say, "Your opening is burying the hook — lead with the story about your grandmother, not the statistics," you have internalized speech structure at a depth that your own practice alone could never reach.

Teaching public speaking involves:
- **Diagnosing specific problems:** Not "you need to be better" but "you are losing the audience during your second point because you are reading from your notes instead of making eye contact"
- **Providing actionable fixes:** Not "use more emotion" but "tell me about your grandmother's garden — now tell the audience that story with the same feeling you just told me"
- **Designing exercises:** Creating practice scenarios tailored to a person's weakness — impromptu drills for someone who freezes, structure exercises for someone who rambles, vocal exercises for someone who mumbles
- **Facilitating practice:** Running a speaking group, managing feedback, creating a safe but challenging environment for growth
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Advanced Speech Forms [Ongoing — One Per Month]
The student writes and delivers one advanced-form speech per month, rotating through: toast, testimony, pitch, eulogy (for a historical figure or fictional scenario), and commencement-style address. Each is delivered to an audience and recorded for review.

### Study 2: Extemporaneous Practice [Weekly]
The student speaks for 5-10 minutes on a topic they know well, using only a three-word outline. Record and review. The goal: 15-minute extemporaneous speaking with coherent structure by the end of the packet.

### Study 3: Great Speech Analysis [Ongoing]
The student reads or listens to one historically significant speech per month and produces a written or oral analysis: What is the structure? What rhetorical techniques are deployed? Where is the storytelling? What makes it effective — or not? What would you change?

### Study 4: Hostile Audience Simulation [At Least Twice]
The mentor and/or other adults role-play a skeptical or oppositional audience. The student delivers a speech on a controversial topic and handles challenges, interruptions, and tough questions with composure. Debrief afterward: what worked, what did not, what to do differently.

### Study 5: Speaking Coach Practicum [Late in the Packet]
The student coaches a younger student or peer through the preparation and delivery of a speech. They provide specific feedback on structure, delivery, and effectiveness. They design and facilitate at least one speaking exercise for a small group.

### Study 6: The Real-Stakes Speech [At Least Once]
The student delivers a speech in a context with genuine consequences: a competition, a public hearing, a pitch to a real panel, a presentation to a professional audience. The transition from practice to performance is where mastery is tested.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Advanced Speech Forms
**Mastery:** The student can write and deliver a toast, eulogy, testimony, and pitch with appropriate register, structure, and emotional calibration for each. The forms feel distinct — the student is not applying the same template to every occasion.

**Not yet ready:** All speeches feel like variations of the five-paragraph speech from Weathering Storms. The student has not internalized the different demands of different forms.

### 2. Extemporaneous Speaking
**Mastery:** The student can speak for 10-15 minutes from a minimal outline with clear structure, specific evidence, and effective delivery. The speech does not feel improvised — it feels prepared, even though it largely is not.

**Not yet ready:** The student loses structure after 3-4 minutes without notes. Points become repetitive. The speech drifts.

### 3. Hostile Audience Management
**Mastery:** The student maintains composure, acknowledges disagreement without conceding, steelmans the opposition, and redirects to their strongest ground. They handle interruptions with grace and humor.

**Not yet ready:** The student becomes visibly flustered, matches hostility with hostility, or caves under pressure and abandons their position.

### 4. Teaching Capability
**Mastery:** The student can diagnose specific speaking problems in others, provide actionable feedback, and design exercises that address identified weaknesses. The person they coach measurably improves.

**Not yet ready:** Feedback is vague ("you did good"), the student cannot identify specific problems, or the coaching does not result in visible improvement in the other person.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Staying in the Comfort Zone
A Standing Ground student with strong Weathering Storms skills may continue giving competent five-minute prepared speeches to friendly audiences and call it mastery. It is not. Push toward advanced forms, unfamiliar audiences, extemporaneous delivery, and real stakes.

### Analyzing Without Practicing
Speech analysis is intellectually engaging and it is tempting to spend more time analyzing great speeches than practicing one's own. Both are necessary. The ratio should favor practice: for every hour spent analyzing someone else's speech, two hours should be spent delivering and reviewing one's own.

### Avoiding the Teaching Component
Coaching others is uncomfortable — it requires a different skill set than performing. The student may resist it or do it superficially. Hold the standard: the coaching must involve specific feedback and measurable improvement in the person coached.

### Rhetorical Skill Without Ethical Grounding
A Standing Ground speaker has real persuasive power. If IC1 formation is incomplete, that power can be deployed manipulatively. Watch for signs: the student who enjoys winning arguments more than finding truth, who uses emotional appeals to distract from weak evidence, or who manipulates rather than persuades. This is a character issue, not a skills issue, and it connects to 3.3.9's explicit treatment of the persuasion-manipulation line.
